socratic.0:
Socrates. You are about to undertake the practical investigation for Unit 4. Tell me plainly: whose work is it?
Student. Mine. I design it, I conduct it, and I analyse it myself.
Socrates. And from where do you draw your question?
Student. From one of three areas — fields, motion or light — and it must involve a single continuous independent variable, so I generate my own primary data.
Socrates. Then how is the labour recorded and finally shown?
Student. Everything goes into a logbook as I work, and I report it as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words.
Socrates. Good. Then let us walk through it in order — what an investigation is, how to measure well, how data become evidence, and how the whole thing is concluded and communicated. Shall we begin?
Student. Lead on.
socratic.1:
Connects Socrates. If you choose a fields question, where will the underlying physics come from?
Student. From Action without contact and Electricity from fields; a light question rests on Light, matter, relativity, and a motion question on the Unit 3 study of motion.
Socrates. So is the physics itself new to you?
Student. No — the physics is old ground. What is new is the discipline of generating evidence and then defending it.
socratic.2:
Socrates. Where, then, does an investigation begin?
Student. With an aim, or a question.
Socrates. Could the question be "is light beautiful?"
Student. No — it must be answerable by measurement, and it must isolate one relationship: how a single continuous quantity depends on another.
Socrates. Name me the roles. The quantity you deliberately change — what is it?
Student. The independent variable. The quantity I measure in response is the dependent variable.
Socrates. And everything else that might meddle?
Student. Each such quantity is a controlled variable, held constant throughout. And here the independent variable must be continuous — able to take any value across a range, not just fixed categories.
socratic.3:
Socrates. Before you collect a single reading, you commit to a prediction. What do we call it, and what must it rest on?
Student. A hypothesis — a testable prediction of the relationship between the independent and dependent variables, stated beforehand and grounded in the relevant physics.
Socrates. Now tell me honestly: do you run the experiment to confirm it?
Student. No — I run it to test it. The data may support it, partially support it, or fail to support it, and I must be willing to accept any of those.
socratic.4:
Socrates. Let us separate two words that students often blur. What is the methodology?
Student. The overall type of investigation — here, a controlled practical experiment that generates primary data.
Socrates. And the method?
Student. The specific procedure: the equipment, the measurements, the range of the independent variable, how many repeated readings I take, and how I analyse it all.
Socrates. You said primary data. Whose data are those?
Student. Mine — data I generate. They can be quantitative, as measured numerical values, or qualitative, as recorded observations.
socratic.5:
Key idea Socrates. When is an experiment valid?
Student. When it investigates what it claims to investigate.
Socrates. And what could rob it of that?
Student. If a controlled variable is let loose, the dependent variable answers to more than one cause; or if my own bias as observer creeps into the readings. Either way the validity is broken.
socratic.6:
Socrates. Now to measuring. Can any measurement be exact?
Student. No measurement is exact.
Socrates. Then what would the true value be?
Student. The value I would find if the quantity could be measured perfectly.
Socrates. And a real reading stands apart from it. What do we name that gap?
Student. The difference between a measurement result and the true value is the measurement error.
socratic.7:
Socrates. Are accuracy and precision the same thing?
Student. No — and treating them as synonyms is a common slip.
Socrates. Define accuracy for me.
Student. It is qualitative: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value. I write "more accurate" or "less accurate" — never an accuracy as a number.
Socrates. And precision?
Student. How close my repeated measurements are to one another — the spread of the readings. It says nothing about closeness to the true value.
Socrates. So can readings be precise yet wrong?
Student. Yes — precise yet inaccurate, or accurate on average yet imprecise. The two travel independently.
socratic.8:
Socrates. An instrument has two further properties, also distinct. What is its resolution?
Student. The smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a perceptible change in the reading — a millimetre ruler resolves to 1 mm, a stopwatch reading hundredths resolves to 0.01 s.
Socrates. And its uncertainty — what does that tell you?
Student. The interval in which the true value is expected to lie. So 20 ± 1 mm means the true value is expected between 19 and 21 mm.
Socrates. By VCE convention, how large is an instrument's uncertainty?
Student. Half its smallest scale increment — so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s.
Socrates. Then may resolution and uncertainty be reported as if they were one quantity?
Student. Never. They are different things and must be kept apart.
socratic.9:
Trap Socrates. A student writes that a millimetre ruler's uncertainty is 1 mm. Where has she erred?
Student. She has used the increment itself. The uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment — the ruler resolves to 1 mm but its uncertainty is only ±0.5 mm.
socratic.10:
Socrates. Errors come in kinds, sorted by how they act on the readings. What is a random error?
Student. An unpredictable variation that scatters readings about the mean. It harms precision, and I tame it by repeating the measurement and averaging.
Socrates. And a systematic error?
Student. It pushes every reading off the true value in the same direction by about the same amount — from calibration, the instrument, or the method, like parallax or a zero error. It harms accuracy.
Socrates. Now the crucial question: will averaging cure both?
Student. Only the random one. Averaging cannot touch a systematic error, because every reading is shifted the same way by the same fault — the average is shifted with them.
socratic.11:
Trap Socrates. If repeating and averaging improves precision, has it fixed everything?
Student. No. It reduces random error, but a consistent offset survives the average untouched.
Socrates. So what must be done with that offset instead?
Student. The fault itself has to be found and removed — averaging will never do it for me.
socratic.12:
Socrates. Repeating measurements lets you make two judgements. What is repeatability?
Student. The closeness of agreement between successive measurements under the same conditions — same observer, instrument, method and place, over a short time. It evaluates precision.
Socrates. And if you change those conditions?
Student. Then I'm testing reproducibility — agreement when a different observer, instrument, method or location is used.
Socrates. Why is that second test so valuable?
Student. Because it checks a claimed precision and can expose a systematic error, tying the result back to accuracy.
socratic.13:
Socrates. And before any of this — before the first reading — what claims your attention?
Student. The health, safety and ethical guidelines: I identify the hazards, control the risks, and conduct the whole investigation responsibly.
socratic.14:
Socrates. We turn now to evidence. What is it?
Student. Data that bear on a hypothesis, a model or a theory, and that may support or refute it.
Socrates. From where do your primary data get their authority?
Student. From how they are recorded — the logbook.
Socrates. Describe it.
Student. A dated, contemporaneous record of every reading, observation, instrument setting and change of plan, written as I go. It proves the data are my own and lets the work be checked.
socratic.15:
Socrates. When you report numbers, the significant-figure conventions bind you. In a sum or difference, what governs the result?
Student. It keeps the fewest decimal places of any value I used.
Socrates. And in a product or quotient?
Student. It keeps the fewest significant figures.
Socrates. When do you round?
Student. Only at the final step — never on intermediate values, so rounding error cannot accumulate.
Socrates. And the symbols and units?
Student. Correct symbols, units and standard abbreviations, in accepted notation — such as m s⁻¹.
socratic.16:
Socrates. How do you make a pattern visible?
Student. By graphing — independent variable on the horizontal axis, dependent variable on the vertical.
Socrates. What does each point carry?
Student. Uncertainty bars: the vertical bar for uncertainty in the dependent variable, the horizontal for the independent, with the true value expected to lie within them.
Socrates. Now the line of best fit — through the first and last points?
Student. No. It follows the trend of all the points, passing within the uncertainty bars. It isn't forced through any chosen point or the origin, and it isn't extended beyond the data.
Socrates. Suppose a line you expect through the origin misses it. What does that reveal?
Student. A systematic error.
socratic.17:
Key idea Socrates. If your relationship curves, must you abandon the straight line?
Student. No — I linearise it. I transform a variable so the curve becomes a straight line, and then the gradient equals a physical quantity.
Socrates. What must travel with the transformed variable?
Student. Its axis units. If I plot t2 against distance, the axis reads "time2 (s2)".
Socrates. And the gradient — how do you take it honestly?
Student. From two points lying on the line, chosen far apart; its units are the vertical-axis units divided by the horizontal-axis units, and I include any ×10n axis scale factor.
socratic.18:
Socrates. A pattern now stands before you. Do you simply accept it?
Student. No — I evaluate it. I identify what caused the uncertainty in my readings, state the assumptions I made and the limitations of the data and method.
Socrates. To what end?
Student. To ask whether those things could account for any departure of my data from the expected relationship.
socratic.19:
Socrates. The conclusion arrives. What must it do?
Student. Answer the question, and state whether the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis.
Socrates. May you write that you have proved your hypothesis?
Student. No — "proves" and "disproves" are forbidden. A single investigation establishes evidence, not proof.
Socrates. And what else belongs there?
Student. The limitations of the investigation, its implications, perhaps a pointer to further work — but no new information that wasn't presented earlier.
socratic.20:
Socrates. Before you settle that conclusion, you owe the work a frank reckoning. What do you weigh?
Student. The limitations of the data and method — sources of random and systematic error, restrictions on the range or number of readings, and assumptions that may not hold.
Socrates. And having named each one?
Student. I propose a realistic improvement for it — not a wish, but something that could actually be done.
socratic.21:
Key idea Socrates. In what form is all this finally judged?
Student. As a scientific poster of no more than 600 words, with prescribed sections.
Socrates. Recite them in order.
Student. The title, which is the investigation question; my name; an introduction giving the aim, the hypothesis or prediction and the background physics; the methodology and methods; the results, shown to reveal the trend; a discussion that interprets and evaluates the data, states limitations and improvements, relates the result to the physics and links back to the hypothesis; a conclusion that answers the question; and the references and acknowledgements.
socratic.22:
Socrates. At the poster's heart sits one thing above all. What is it?
Student. A single Communication statement — one sentence answering the investigation question.
Socrates. How prominent?
Student. It takes up between 20 and 25 per cent of the poster's area, so a reader grasps the central finding at a glance.
Socrates. And the poster around it?
Student. It follows the conventions of science communication — correct terminology, symbols, equations, standard abbreviations, significant figures and units — and acknowledges its references succinctly.
socratic.23:
Connects Socrates. Give me a few questions you might actually pose.
Student. A fields one — how magnetic field strength varies with distance from a wire, drawing on Action without contact; a motion one — how a projectile's range depends on launch angle; a light one — how fringe spacing depends on slit separation, drawing on Light, matter, relativity.
Socrates. However different they look, what stays constant across them all?
Student. The discipline of this Area of Study — one continuous independent variable, controlled conditions, honest uncertainty. That never changes, whatever the question.
Socrates. Then you are ready to begin. Go and measure well.
